nodes.description: KCC3 loss in neurons produces abnormal motoneuron electrical activity, presynaptic neurotransmission defects, neuromuscular junction abnormalities, denervation, and muscle atrophy in model systems, connecting KCC3 loss to progressive motor impairment.

evidence.reference_title: KCC3 loss-of-function contributes to Andermann syndrome by inducing activity-dependent neuromuscular junction defects.

neuron-specific KCC3 loss-of-function in mice leads to early neuromuscular
junction (NMJ) abnormalities and muscular atrophy

nodes.description: KCC3 normally mediates electroneutral potassium and chloride efflux in neurons, contributing to cell volume and intracellular chloride homeostasis. Loss of this function disturbs ion homeostasis in neurons and peripheral nerve fibers.
nodes.id: Impaired neuronal potassium-chloride cotransport and cell-volume regulation

evidence.reference_title: A role for KCC3 in maintaining cell volume of peripheral nerve fibers.
evidence.snippet:
KCC3 is responsible for
the efflux of K+ and Cl- in neurons to help maintain cell volume and
intracellular chloride levels.

nodes.description: KCC3 deficiency disrupts peripheral nerve conduction and produces a progressive central and peripheral axonopathy. Autopsy tissue shows developmental commissural abnormalities together with axonomas and axonal degeneration affecting peripheral and central axons.
nodes.id: Peripheral axonopathy and nerve conduction failure

and the nerve conduction velocity was slower in nerves from KCC3(-/-) mice than
in nerves from wild-type mice

evidence.reference_title: Novel truncating and missense mutations of the KCC3 gene associated with Andermann syndrome.
evidence.snippet:
CONCLUSIONS: Not only truncating but also missense mutations of the KCC3 gene
are associated with Andermann syndrome. Different types of KCC3 mutations may

nodes.description: Biallelic SLC12A6 pathogenic variants impair or abolish KCC3 potassium-chloride cotransporter function. Functional assays of disease variants show nonfunctional membrane-expressed or mislocalized transporter, establishing loss of KCC3 activity as the upstream molecular lesion.

evidence.reference_title: The K-Cl cotransporter KCC3 is mutant in a severe peripheral neuropathy associated with agenesis of the corpus callosum.
evidence.snippet:
the truncated mutant is appropriately glycosylated and expressed at the cellular
membrane, where it is non-functional.

type, the mutant protein was mislocalized to the cytoplasm, disrupting its ion
transport function.
